description: The quantifier expressing the larger portion of a whole—more than half but explicitly not all. 'Shelami' acknowledges that majorities matter for collective decisions and practical planning, while its distinction from 'theula' (all) preserves space for dissent and exception. This is the grammar of democratic thinking: the greater part speaks, but does not erase the remainder.
sound_symbolism: The word begins with 'she', the soft fricative opening establishing kinship with its sibling 'sheloi' (many) in the realm of large quantities. The liquid 'la' follows, gathering and flowing as portions accumulate past the halfway mark. Finally, 'mi' closes on the high, focused vowel—the greater share arrived at, singled out from the whole. Speaking 'shelami' enacts the gathering of majority: spreading abundance, flowing together, arriving at predominance.
grammatical_notes: Functions as a quantifier preceding nouns: 'shelami miona' (most people), 'shelami philo' (most days). Implies comparison to a whole—there must be a reference set from which the majority is drawn. Distinguished from 'sheloi' (many) by implying proportion rather than mere abundance: 'sheloi' says 'a large number' while 'shelami' says 'the larger part.' When the reference set is contextually clear, no explicit mention is needed. Never combines with the plural particle 'lo'—the quantifier itself implies plurality.

pillars.solarpunk-values: Collective decision-making requires acknowledging majorities without erasing minorities. 'Shelami' supports the democratic processes that regenerative communities need—honoring what most want while protecting space for alternative visions.
pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Majorities are patterns of the present moment, not eternal truths. 'Shelami' allows speaking of predominance while remembering that today's majority may be tomorrow's minority—all formations are impermanent, including collective opinions.
pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: By distinguishing 'most' from 'all,' Phi builds grammatical protection for minorities. 'Shelami' acknowledges predominant views while linguistically preserving space for those who differ—the language itself resists the tyranny of unanimity.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Traditional councils often sought consensus but recognized that perfect agreement is rare. 'Shelami' serves the practical wisdom of acting on majority understanding while remaining open to dissenting voices that may prove prophetic.
